text: Robotic approach in morbidly obese patients with low rectal cancer may confer advantages over laparoscopic approach. This is a fifty-year-old male patient, diagnosed case of stage III low rectal cancer, received neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy, underwent surgery ten weeks post-treatment. His BMI was forty. He had a palpable rectal mass five centimeters from anal verge located posteriorly. He had an endoscopy done, which showed a lesion, as shown in the picture. His biopsy showed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. This patient showed good response to the chemoradiotherapy and had a palpable ridge at the previous site of tumor. We have pre-treatment MRI image as shown in the picture. We have post-chemoradiotherapy MRI image. CT staging revealed no metastasis. MDT decision was to proceed with surgery.

The robotic XI system is utilized with integrated table motion for the procedure. The diagram illustrates standard port placement for robotic TME procedures, including an additional 5mm port for assistant access. The patient is positioned head down at 22 degrees with a 7 to 9-degree right side tilt. The greater omentum is placed above the transverse colon, and the small bowel is moved medially using gauze to expose the medial side of the mesentery. The dissection begins at the sacral promontory, adopting a medial to lateral approach.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric artery at its root and secures it with a white vascular stapler. A medial to lateral approach is used to develop the plane cranially, staying anterior to Girota's fascia while protecting the ureter and gonadal vessels. The inferior mesenteric vein is isolated and clipped below the pancreas to facilitate mesentery lengthening and tension-free anastomosis. The pancreas serves as a landmark for accessing the lesser sac, aiding in splenic flexure mobilization. Lateral mobilization is performed with monopolar diathermy along the white line, using gauze to prevent small bowel injury. The splenic flexure is routinely mobilized, with the plane developed above the colon, separating the greater omentum in the embryological plane. The patient is positioned head up to drop the colon and open the lesser sac, providing a clear view of the pancreas. Dissection at the mesotransverse level minimizes marginal artery injury risk, and complete release of the splenic flexure is achieved.

The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) by dissecting between the hypogastric nerves at the back of the superior rectal artery, using gentle traction and monopolar diathermy. The assistant uses suction to reduce smoke from the diathermy. The dissection progresses posteriorly, staying on the yellow side of the white layers. The focus then shifts to the right lateral side, staying behind the seminal vesicle, and involves cutting the cuff of peritoneum held by the robotic left hand for anterior dissection. The dissection proceeds to the left of the rectum, developing a plane either outside or underneath the Denonvilliers fascia, anteriorly.

The surgeon clearly visualizes the left hypogastric nerve plexus while continuing dissection from the left pararectal space to connect with the previously established plane below the seminal vesicle. The dissection progresses anally under the seminal vesicle and behind the Denonvilliers fascia to minimize the risk of damaging the hypogastric nerve.

The surgeon approaches the pelvic floor on the right side, joining previously created planes both anteriorly and posteriorly. The same steps are repeated on the left side of the specimen. The anococcygeal ligament is released from the lower third of the rectum, facilitating stapling. A stapling device is introduced through the twelve millimeter arm number three, aiming to divide the lower rectum from twelve to six o'clock.

The surgeon performs two stapler firings using a green cartridge on the mobilized rectal tube. The first staple line is followed by a second, ensuring the staple line extends past the lower end of the tube. A CDH-XXIX anastomosis is completed at the coloanal junction, protected by a covering loop ileostomy.
